chunk_summary: Micronutrient planning for selective eating disorders focuses on addressing multi-micronutrient deficiencies in children with neurodisability or ASD, using targeted dietary interventions, testing, and supplementation strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the common micronutrient deficiencies in children with selective eating disorders.
Learn how to assess dietary patterns and identify missing food groups in high-risk populations.
Develop targeted testing and supplementation strategies for selective eaters.
Recognize when to refer for feeding therapy or further clinical evaluation.
key_facts: Iron deficiency is common due to limited meat/legume intake; calcium and vitamin D deficiency often result from dairy avoidance and low sun exposure.
Vitamin B12 deficiency is associated with vegetarian or low animal-source diets; zinc deficiency occurs occasionally.
Baseline labs may include CBC, ferritin paired with CRP, and thyroid screening if symptoms suggest; vitamin D testing is reserved for high-risk cases.
Supplementation should use single-nutrient or clearly dosed combined products with a defined stop/review date (8–12 weeks).
Avoid indefinite high-dose multivitamins due to toxicity risks and drug–nutrient interactions.
Nonresponse to interventions may indicate malabsorption, chronic inflammation, or behavioral barriers requiring feeding therapy.
reference_quote: Children with neurodisability or ASD-associated selective eating often present with behavioral concerns plus growth faltering rather than 'classic' deficiency signs. (Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne)
document_key_topics: Micronutrient deficiencies in selective eating disorders
Referral for feeding therapy in nonresponsive cases
